data.title: Svenska Handelsbanken: Credit Can Be A Differentiator
data.description:
For all the drama surrounding U.S. trade policy, European financials continue to enjoy an excellent 2025, with the sector now up well over 40% in U.S. dollar terms. While it has slightly lagged, this rising tide has nevertheless been a boon for Sweden's Svenska Handelsbanken (OTCPK:SVNLF) (OTCPK:SVNLY), with Handelsbanken's ADSs returning around 36% so far this year, including a ~14% return since my last update in March.

Looking back, my bull case for Handelsbanken contained a few moving parts. Above the provisioning line, while the bank was seeing headwinds from lower interest rates, fee income growth and a much-welcome efficiency drive was at least providing a degree of support to earnings. Below it, well, credit quality has always been a standout feature of Handelsbanken. Given normalizing asset quality is a question of "when" rather than "if" for European banks, I figure this one is going too far better than most. Finally, Handelsbanken is sitting on one of the healthiest capital cushions in the space, and that could provide additional support to its dividend as earnings normalize.

With the above in mind, Handelsbanken's first quarter was reassuring, with the bank delivering more progress on fee growth and efficiency alongside another net credit release. While credit has always been a differentiator here, it has arguably gained even greater salience since my last update, with this period obviously encompassing the sweeping U.S. tariffs announced on "Liberation Day". Given Handelsbanken should be one of the least trade-sensitive European names I cover, I remain bullish.

Efficiency And Credit Continue To Provide Support

Having surged alongside rising interest rates, Handelsbanken's income continues to normalize now that policy rates are falling. As a remainder, Handelsbanken's revenue skews over 75% to net interest income. This is one of the highest shares among the European banks I cover and makes Handelsbanken relatively sensitive to changes in rates.

Last quarter, Handelsbanken generated SEK 11.347 billion in NII, down just over 3% sequentially. While day count negatively impacted the comp, NII was also down 2% year-on-year. Although Handelsbanken can't fully offset this, I have been looking it for to mitigate this headwind in two ways. Firstly, to grow its non-interest revenue, thereby limiting the decline in NII on overall income. Although NII dominates its top line, Handelsbanken still has good non-income lines of business, including in asset management, where it has a leading share of mutual fund inflows in Sweden.

This continues to play out. Last quarter, fee income rose by 5% year-on-year to SEK 2.9 billion, with fee income in the savings business rising 7% to nearly SEK 2 billion.

I have also been looking for support below the revenue line, namely in costs. Per the chart below, this had been a weak spot for the bank, with Handelsbanken previously displaying one of the worst cost-to-income trends among large Nordic peers.

Progress continues on this front. Last quarter, Handelsbanken reported operating costs of SEK 6.025 billion, down 7% on the year-ago period. Staff numbers declined by the same amount. While there would usually be a risk that personnel cuts could impact revenue, these cuts are not taking place at the branch-level, which remains key to Handelsbanken's business model.

As a result of these trends, pre-provision income has held up relatively well, falling by less than 1% year-on-year last quarter to SEK 8.763 billion.

Credit quality also continues to support earnings. Indeed, Handelsbanken actually recorded a modest SEK 54 million release last quarter (~1bp of total loans), its fifth straight period of positive provisioning. Credit has historically been one of the strongest aspects of this bank. Per the chart below, Handelsbanken simply doesn't lose much on the loans that it makes, even during extreme stress periods like the 2007-2009 financial crisis.

As you can see from the image below, residential and commercial mortgages account for the vast majority of Handelsbanken's loan book. Other forms of consumer and business lending are relatively limited.

Credit quality remains sound, with only 0.33% of Handelsbanken's loans non-performing last quarter. Of course, defaults are just one aspect of credit losses, as Handelsbanken can also recover sour debt through collateral. This also looks solid. In Sweden, Handelsbanken's weighted average loan-to-value ratio is around 60% in retail mortgages (and less on commercial mortgages). It is similarly conservative in its other markets like the Netherlands and the U.K., affording the bank a large collateral cushion should defaults rise.

Given U.S. tariffs, this feature of Handelsbanken's business has arguably taken on greater importance since my last update. While European banks with larger corporate exposure could see meaningful downside to their earnings in a bad case outcome, this is less of a risk for Handelsbanken.

Furthermore, Handelsbanken's balance sheet remains in excellent shape, with its CET1 Capital Ratio clocking in at 18.4% last quarter. This is 350bps above its regulatory minimum and 150bps above the mid-point of management's target range, leaving the door open for additional capital returns to shareholders.

Handelsbanken remains reasonably valued. As I type, its shares trade for SEK 128.05 ($6.67 per ADS), equal to 1.38x book value per share as of Q1. Per below, Handelsbanken has historically averaged a ~13% return on equity. Assuming a similar performance ahead, that would put its shares at a P/E of around 10.6x based on through-the-cycle profitability.

While that makes Handelsbanken slightly more expensive than some other European banks I follow, Handelsbanken's strong balance sheet and lower credit risk to tariffs warrant a premium. Moreover, Handelsbanken continues to offer a very high payout ratio. It paid out SEK 15 per share in respect of fiscal 2024, mapping to a yield of 11.7% and a payout ratio of 108% of EPS. Its above-average yield potential provides additional support to the stock.

Conclusion

Against contracting NII, Handelsbanken continues to deliver growing fee income and cost control, supporting its earnings. Credit quality has long been one of the strongest aspects of this bank. Given concerns that European economies may suffer as a result of U.S. tariffs, this feature of Handelsbanken's business may take on greater significance in the quarters ahead. Alongside its strong balance sheet and reasonable valuation, this keeps me bullish.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: ioneer: Sodium-Ion Overhangs Lithium Market (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
When I last covered ioneer Ltd (NASDAQ:IONR) (OTCPK:GSCCF) in early February, a bit of life seemed to have crept back into the lithium market. After years of constantly declining prices, the metal appeared to have bottomed-out last October, and shortly after, lithium investors began experiencing something that they hadn't seen in a long, long time: month-over-month price increases.

As discussed in my article, this new phenomenon was soon followed by reports prophesizing the imminent end of the global lithium surplus, with some of the more enthusiastic market watchers even going so far as to forecast a small deficit in 2026. Needless to say, the news provided long-suffering lithium investors with some respite. Although, it’s probably safe to assume that the collective mood was already improving as the new administration aggressively pushes for the US to adopt a more nationalistic policy of natural resource production, something that would likely benefit ioneer’s stock price.

However, no sooner had lithium investors begun breathing a little easier when news coming out of China appeared to set lithium prices upon a downward march, once again.

The Lithium Market

To be sure, there are still plenty of voices in the lithium sector forecasting surpluses this year and next. The above-mentioned 2026 deficit call was made by one shop, Benchmark Mineral Intelligence, and it only foresees a marginal deficit in 2026. All the other major analysts maintain their surplus forecasts for both 2025 and 2026, and this week, Wood Mackenzie joined the chorus when it published a research note predicting the surplus might last for a few more years.

But while analysts may disagree as to when the surpluses might turn into deficits, most appear to believe that the market will eventually tip back into deficit as demand for EVs and batteries continues to expand. And that's certainly a reasonable presupposition, and it's also good news for ioneer shareholders as the company’s Rhyolite Ridge project is only expected to begin operations in 2028 anyways, so volatility in the lithium market between now and then will have a more limited impact on the company.

However, those long-term forecasts may need to be updated after CATL, the world’s leading EV battery manufacturer with a 38% market share, announced plans to soon begin mass-producing sodium-ion batteries. That's a potentially negative development for lithium producers, as sodium-ion batteries don’t require any lithium.

The development of sodium-ion batteries is nothing new, they have been around for years and have been the subject of innumerable optimistic announcements by various industry players. But what makes this case different are CATL’s firm plans to start mass-producing its Naxtra battery in relatively short order. This was not the announcement of some breakthrough in a lab but rather, a signal that the world's largest battery producer is ready to put meaningful amounts of CapEx into sodium-ion production.

And while the lithium sector’s reaction to the news has been fairly muted, the lithium price appears to have responded as one would expect. In mid-March, reports began surfacing of CATL’s intentions to begin mass-production and when the company made the formal announcement on April 21, lithium prices began to tumble. Granted, one can never state with exact certainty as to what information caused a fall in price, but the two occurrences appear to be highly correlated.

ioneer’s Boron Production

Needless to say, the appearance of a sodium-ion product in the battery space is not a positive development for lithium producers such as ioneer. And it’s the reason for my downgrading IONR’s stock from a Buy to a Hold rating. But by the same token, the introduction of sodium will probably not spell the end for lithium.

Lithium-ion batteries continue to have greater energy density than their sodium cousin, making them the go-to choice for higher performance EVs. Lithium also continues to be a strategically important resource of which the United States produces comparatively little, strengthening the national security case for Rhyolite Ridge’s development. After all, forecasted production from the mine would quadruple US lithium production.

But there’s another important, and often ignored, reason why the Rhyolite Ridge project will probably get built and operate for many years. IONR is often hyped as being a lithium project, but what’s often forgotten is that it will generate up to 40% of its revenue from boron. The reason why that's often ignored is that boron is boring, and that’s a good thing.

Boron has a very wide range of applications; it's used in products ranging from detergents to glass and, as discussed in a previous article on USA Rare Earth (USAR), is critical in the production of NdFeB magnets, which are used for electric vehicles, wind turbines, and some advanced defense systems. Its current price is about $733/MT, and it tends to experience much less price volatility than many other commodities. And while the US does produce some boron, Turkey dominates global production with a 70% market share.

Boron’s wide range of applications, concentrated production by a potential geopolitical competitor, and relative price stability has not been lost on ioneer’s management. They consistently try to get the message across that Rhyolite Ridge is a ‘Lithium-Boron Project’ with a Reserve of 60Mt grading over 15kppm Boron.

Takeaway

But given the wild lithium price swings of recent years and the fact that most of IONR’s revenue will be derived from lithium, it comes as no surprise that investors have focused on those operations. Changes in the lithium market are the reason why I'm downgrading the stock to a Hold, but in my opinion, its boron operations help to de-risk the project and will help it get to commercial production.

data.title: MLP Risk And Return Vs. Other Energy Assets
data.description:
Everyone knows that master limited partnerships (MLPs) aren’t your typical energy investments. Their pass-through tax structure, large quarterly distributions, and focus on midstream assets like pipelines set them apart from upstream oil explorers or downstream refiners.

But beyond those structural differences, what really matters is the bottom line: how do MLPs compare on risk and return over time? Have they actually been the better part of the energy sector to invest in for the long haul? We think the data makes a strong case. Here’s why.

MLPs Have Outperformed with Less Risk

The Alerian MLP Index (AMZ) tracks midstream master limited partnerships, which generate most of their revenue by transporting and storing oil, gas, and refined products.

The S&P Energy Select Sector Index (IXE) is a market-cap weighted benchmark of large U.S. energy companies, dominated by integrated majors like Exxon Mobil (XOM) and Chevron (CVX).

The S&P Oil & Gas Equipment Select Industry Index (SPOGEQSI) captures oilfield services and drilling contractors, names like Schlumberger (SLB), Baker Hughes (BKR).

Meanwhile, the S&P Oil & Gas Exploration & Production Index (SPOGEPSI) is made up of upstream energy firms like ConocoPhillips (COP) focused on finding and extracting oil and gas.

Over the five-year window ending in May 2025, a period marked by extreme dislocations, from the COVID-19 oil collapse to Russia’s invasion of Ukraine and ongoing conflict in the Middle East, MLPs outperformed across the board.

AMZ delivered a 26.98% annualized total return with just 24.13% volatility. That’s a stronger result than IXE’s 21.23% return and 29.87% volatility, and far more favorable than the higher-risk, lower-return profiles of SPOGEQSI and SPOGEPSI.

Why MLPs Are Structurally More Solid

Ceteris paribus, meaning all else equal (there are always exceptions), we believe MLPs are positioned to continue offering a mix of attractive returns and relatively lower volatility thanks to some structural advantages built into their business models.

The energy sector is no stranger to turbulence. For companies involved in exploration and production, revenues are highly sensitive to commodity prices. If oil prices drop, their top line gets hit almost immediately, as the product they sell is now worth less.

Downstream refiners and chemical firms, while sometimes cushioned by refining margins, also face risk from narrowing spreads, fluctuating input costs, and demand swings. Volumes and pricing can both be volatile, and that variability flows straight into the financial statements.

Now contrast that with the typical MLP. These businesses operate energy infrastructure: pipelines, storage tanks, and terminals. Rather than profiting off commodity prices directly, they earn fees based on volumes transported or stored, often through long-term, fee-based contracts.

That means if oil prices drop, but producers still need to move product, pipeline operators keep collecting. Cash flow is steadier, more visible, and more contractually locked in than in many other parts of the energy sector. That reliable income stream is what allows MLPs to pay above-average distributions and keep volatility lower.

Of course, MLPs are not low risk by any means. They face regulatory scrutiny, especially around environmental impact, and pipelines can become political flashpoints. Changes in tax policy or shifts in energy transition sentiment can affect valuations. And like any other asset, poor management or overleveraged can erode investor returns.

But on the whole, for investors seeking exposure to the energy sector without the boom-bust rollercoaster of producers or oil services, MLPs offer a strong alternative. You still get commodity-linked upside, but through a steadier toll booth model, one that historically has paid you to stay the course.

Disclosure

This information is not an offer to sell, or solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product, security, or services offered by Jay Hatfield, or Infrastructure Capital Advisors, LLC, (“ICA”) or its affiliates. ICA, will only conduct such solicitation of an offer to buy any investment product or service offered by ICA, if at all, by (1) purported definitive documentation (which will include disclosures relating to investment objective, policies, risk factors, fees, tax implications and relevant qualifications), (2) to qualified participants, if applicable, and (3) only in those jurisdictions where permitted by law. Jay Hatfield or ICA may have a beneficial long or short position in securities discussed either through stock ownership, options, or other derivatives; nonetheless, under no circumstances does any article or interview represent a recommendation to buy or sell these securities. This discussion is intended to provide insight into stocks and the market for entertainment and information purposes only and is not a solicitation of any kind. ICA buys and sells securities on behalf of its fund investors and may do so, before and after any particular article herein is published, with respect to the securities discussed in any article posted. ICA's appraisal of a company (price target) is only one factor that affects its decision whether to buy or sell shares in that company. Other factors might include, but are not limited to, the presence of mandatory limits on individual positions, decisions regarding portfolio exposures, and general market conditions and liquidity needs. As such, there may not always be consistency between the views expressed here and ICA's trading or holdings on behalf of its fund investors. There may be conflicts between the content posted or discussed and the interests of ICA. Please reach out to the ICA for more information. Investors should make their own decisions regarding any investments mentioned, and their prospects based on such investors’ own review of publicly available information and should not rely on the information contained herein. ICA nor any of its affiliates accepts any liability whatsoever for any direct or consequential loss howsoever arising, directly or indirectly, from any use of the information contained herein. We have not sought, nor have we received, permission from any third-party to include their information in this article. Certain information contained in this document constitutes “forward-looking statements,” which can be identified by the use of forward-looking terminology such as “may,” “will,” “should,” “expect,” “anticipate,” “project,” “estimate,” “intend,” “continue” or “believe” or the negatives thereof or other variations thereon or other comparable terminology. Due to various risks and uncertainties, actual events or results may differ materially from those reflected or contemplated in such forward-looking statements. The information contained herein represents our subjective belief and opinions and should not be construed as investment, tax, legal, or financial advice.

data.title: USDCAD Approaches Weekly Lows: Where Could The Loonie Go From Here?
data.description:
By Elior Manier

USDCAD had seen a decent rebound from last week lows, starting the week at 1.36850.

It was a generally positive week for the US Dollar and a relatively muted one for the Loonie. A lack of key economic data before this morning led to some recovery in the USD, particularly as earnings came in overall better than expected.

We just received the report for the Core PCE Data, which came in as expected, at 2.5% Y/Y.

This piece of data is what the Fed wants to see to resume the cut cycle that had begun in September 2024, taking the Effective Fed Funds Rate (The main US Policy rate) from 5.50% to the current 4.25%.

The last 25 bps cut was done in December 2024.

The Bank of Canada, on the other hand, was cutting rates aggressively from June 2024 to March 2025 and paused at the last meeting - taking the Policy Rate from 5% to the current 2.75%.

The Central Banks and the market are now waiting for further influence from the Trump tariffs to implement more changes to their stance, as USDCAD had gone from 1.3450 to 1.4750 between October 2024 and February 2025.

Observe the key levels for the pair through a multi-timeframe technical analysis.

USDCAD Technical Analysis - From the Daily to the Hourly Timeframes

USDCAD Daily Chart

USDCAD has been a volatile pair, to say the least, as we’ve seen many themes adding to the depreciation of the Canadian Dollar: the aggressive rate cuts from the BoC, tariffs on key sectors, including Steel and Aluminum production - one of the key producing sectors for Canada - and unforeseen trade tensions between the two neighboring nations.

USDCAD is now moving toward levels last seen in October 2024 and towards what has before been a more typical range for the pair, between 1.30 and 1.40.

Prices are moving towards the main daily support between 1.3686 and 1.3730. The next daily support is 1.3550 - 1.3600.

If prices were to rebound from here, look toward the Main Daily Resistance zone: 1.3750 to 1.38. A break of that would hint toward the next Daily Resistance at 1.41 - 1.4150.

USDCAD 4H Chart

Prices have just gone through the preceding S1 zone that was located between 1.3780 and 1.3800, having rejected the highs after yesterday's US Federal Court announcement to terminate Trump's plan to impose tariffs - what has been named the Trump Taco.

Since USD weakness has been observed throughout different markets, the USDCAD has also been influenced by that theme. Trump has also spoken more about the Canada Annexation theme with his plans for a Golden Dome, though those are just some of the several foolhardy quotes from the US President.

Prices are now approaching S2 levels between 1.3740 and 1.3760.

USDCAD 1H Chart

The 1h Chart takes a deeper look at where prices could stall. The MA 50 on the hourly timeframe is actually adding more pressure in the rejection, pointing more towards S3 levels between 1.37 and 1.3720.

If prices break further, look towards prices stalling in the heart of the Daily Support Level, as we can observe a technical confluence with the high of the descending channel.

Any rebound from here hints at the Main Pivot situated at 1.38150.

Safe Trades!

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: The U.S. Government Debt And Deficit Are Symptoms Of A Much Bigger Problem
data.description:
Moody’s Downgraded the US… Should You Care?

The S&P downgraded the US credit rating in 2011. Then, in 2023, Fitch followed suit. Finally, Moody's pulled the trigger on May 16, 2025. Their decision followed a review of the latest White House proposal - code name "One Big Beautiful Bill" - adding $3.3T in new debt over the next 10 years.

For context, the stock market dropped 7% after the S&P downgrade. The Fitch downgrade led to a more muted 2.3% decline. This time, the stock market didn’t flinch - but the bond market showed irritation, with the 30-year yield rising above 5%.

But should you be concerned?

The 2025 budget, passed in April, already entails a $1.9T deficit this year. Total debt stands at $36.7T. What’s another $0.33T per year, incrementally?

Whether you like or dislike the Trump administration, this has little to do with Trump. If President Trump doesn't spend more, we believe the next POTUS will.

Who would stop the music and spoil the party? And to what end? Who are we really hurting with all this debt - debt we neither plan nor need to repay?

The US Technically Can’t Default. How Does the Massive Debt Matter Then?

To be sure, the US would not actually default on its debt! US debts are issued in USD. The US is uniquely skilled at manufacturing the USD - that is our biggest comparative advantage, after all! We can always print more USD to pay.

We don’t believe the credit downgrading is about "US default risk." It warns of the decline in the value of the dollar - driven by ever-larger government bureaucracy and worsening efficiency. We believe the math of currency debasement is straightforward.

When the state bureaucracy doles out financial wealth through pork and welfare - often creating more wealth for the administrative machine than the recipients of the welfare - we find no corresponding real production occurs. As the government grants wealth, it essentially gives consumption rights without earned contribution. This usurps the wealth that other American workers have earned.

On the other hand, if the government spends wisely on critical infrastructure, education or research, the resulting productivity gain is deflationary. For that reason, good government policy often drives strong USD.

In downgrading the US, the rating agencies signal their judgment: federal spending is increasingly wasteful, and the expanding role of government risk crowding out the private sector. A higher inflation and a weakening USD is therefore the future.

Back to First Principles: What Is “Money Printing”?

Before we get too far, let’s be absolutely clear, when economists talk about "printing money," they don't mean the actual production of the dollar bills by the Bureau of Engraving and Printing to replace worn out bills.

In reality, when we say the Fed “can print money” we mean it is able to borrow, at will, an unlimited amount of money from the big banks, which hold the collective savings of Americans. The Fed also gets to “choose” the rate it borrows at and when it repays the debts.

The Fed: Enabler of the Government’s Spending Addiction

With potentially infinite access to money, the Fed is the lender of last resort - not just to troubled banks during financial crises but to the US government when other lenders are reluctant. When push comes to shove, the Fed can and always has “bailed” out the spending addiction of our political rulers.

Thus, the US government cannot technically default on its debt servicing. When facing maturing debts, it simply issues new bonds, which the Fed stands ready to buy with its unlimited credit card! The government’s debts and interests are easily rolled over.

Once you understand the mechanism, you shouldn’t be surprised that the government has $36.7T in debt going on $40T. With unlimited credit and infinite refi - you too will owe $36.7T.

An Independent Fed? A Good Theory at Best

Technically, the Fed is independent. But its chairman and board of governors are appointed by the POTUS. While the POTUS can’t fire a Fed Chair, he certainly has leverage.

Frankly, no one is appointed the Fed Chair without a track record of having “played ball” well. Counting on the Fed to be the adult in the room - that it will discipline politicians by withholding funding for pork and welfare - is idealistic.

Argentina has had a long list of short-lived Central Bankers. It most certainly is not because they repeatedly pick bad economists who can’t manage inflation. He who refused to print to fund the president didn’t survive long on the job.

Sadly, neither the debt ceiling nor the Fed independence has credibility in stopping our political rulers from spending more.

How Much Debt Is Too Much?

The government’s unlimited credit card is backed by the collective balance sheet of all Americans. The tipping point comes when liabilities exceed assets.

The press often cites the US debt-to-GDP, now 130% ($36.7T/$28T). But comparing debt to income misses the broader picture. A better metric is debt-to-wealth.

US national wealth is estimated at $170T. With annual income of $28T and tax receipts of $5T, the government spends $7T - resulting in a $2T deficit. Households save about $1T per year.

Terrifyingly, the government spends $1T more than we collectively save. Thankfully, rising asset values (stocks and real estate) have offset this imbalance.

The US debt-to-wealth ratio is about 20%. For comparison:

  • Japan: $8.6T debt / $27T wealth = 32%
  • UK: $3.9T / $16.4T = 24%
  • Greece: $0.4T / $1.1T = 36%
  • Argentina: $0.46T / $1.2T = 38% (improved from 45% prior to Milei)

Has the Debt Grown Out of Control?

Thirty years ago, US government debt was $3.6T. Today, it’s $36.7T. But national wealth also rose tenfold, from $17T to $170T. Debt-to-wealth ratio is unchanged at ~20%.

We think some body fat is fine - necessary, in fact. But we shouldn’t be obese. At 20% "body fat," we’re tolerable. But we should be ever vigilant regarding the trajectory.

For now, there is, perhaps, an organic awareness - a built-in restraint that keeps the government from driving the economy into ruin.

A sensible parasite doesn’t kill its host.

Debt and Deficits Are Signals, Not Causes

Let me prove to you that the government deficit is not the primary concern.

Let’s tax an extra $2T to eliminate the US budget deficit. However, we fund the exact same government expenditure - including all the same waste, welfare, and pork. Have we solved the real problem? Economic theory would predict similar inflation and growth drag.

The question isn’t how we fund inefficiency - it’s whether the inefficiency itself is acceptable.

The government invests in schools, infrastructure, basic scientific research, law and order - things which aren’t naturally undertaken by private for-profit companies. However, most people suspect that a substantial amount of the budgeted spending is wasteful: bloated welfare state and pork.

Looking naively at our deficit would misguide us on the bigger problem: the government - this inefficient non-profit entity has grown from an iguana to a Komodo dragon. If we aren’t careful, it could become Godzilla.

Japan, UK, and the US run government deficits of 3.5%, 5%, and 6% of GDP, respectively. The US ranks poorly here. Deficits mislead you here. Let’s look at true government imposition:

  • Japan's government spends $1.7T of its $4.2T GDP → 41% (stable over 20 years)
  • UK: $1.7T of $3.8T → 44% (worsened rapidly in the last five years from ~30%)
  • US: $7T of $28T → 25% (deteriorated in the last five years from ~20%)

In terms of government footprint, the US still looks relatively reasonable for now!

At its worst, Greek government spending hit 70% of GDP. Argentina peaked at 140% and averaged 80% until Milei brought it below 40%.

Do not let deficits and debt - which are financing problems - distract you from the real problem. In our opinion, good firms don’t fail because they choose to finance their bad projects with external debt instead of internal equity capital. Good firms fail because they keep funding failing divisions.

Ironically, a large deficit - a visible warning sign that forces public awareness - might help. It reminds politicians: the public won’t pay more taxes to fund bloated programs.

Government: A Necessary Evil?

What can we do? Perhaps not much - except hope.

A well-behaved parasite coexists with its host. Argentina has shown what happens when that balance is broken. The host dies. So does the parasite.

For now, for the US, there is no threat of bankruptcy to deter government spending. We predict Moody’s or the “bond vigilante” will, in due course, be overruled by the Fed, who ultimately serves the US government. To be sure, pork and welfare will keep flowing. The parasite is still mindful not to kill the host.

Back to the original question: Who are we really hurting? Wise men have said in different tongues and in different words: Life is not fair. Get over it. If you are a red blood cell or T-cell, simply be glad that the body will live and even thrive, despite it all.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Nvidia Q1 Earnings Review: China Restrictions Getting Worse
data.description:
Co-Authored by Noah Cox and Brock Heilig

Investment Thesis

GPU giant Nvidia (NASDAQ:NVDA), one of the world’s largest technology companies, has experienced incredible growth both in its stock price and in its earnings and revenues over the past two years on the back of an AI revolution that has had no sign of stopping. Nvidia has worked its way into the top three of assets in the world (in terms of market cap), only behind Gold and Microsoft.

Despite this, I think it's becoming clear that Nvidia’s hypergrowth phase is clearly cooling. Revenue of $44.1 billion in FQ1 rose 69% year-over-year, which pales in comparison to 262% year-over-year growth a year ago in the same quarter, and just 12% sequentially - its slowest QoQ increase since the AI boom began.

In my opinion (and as CEO Jensen Huang said on Wednesday), U.S. export curbs on H20 chips are a material drag. The company wrote down $4.5 billion of excess inventory and forgone orders and lost ~$2.5 billion in unshipped China sales, given that market historically accounted for approximately 13% of revenue.

I continue to be concerned about the stock’s steep multiple given rapidly slowing growth. The company currently trades at about 32x forward earnings and roughly 25x trailing sales. Both of these numbers are huge premiums to peers (sector median). This leaves little room for error if growth normalizes further.

With this, I’m still bearish on the AI giant. While it’s clear that Nvidia still leads AI accelerators, it's clear from the earnings data that the company’s sprint is turning into a brisk jog. Because of this, I still believe that shares of the technology leader are a strong sell.

Why I’m Doing Follow Up Coverage

Earlier this last week, I pegged shares of Nvidia as a strong sell ahead of Q1 earnings in my latest piece of coverage. In that article, I focused on what I think is their elevated valuation (~32x forward P/E) and the looming export restrictions could dent earnings. Since earnings, these have only gotten worse.

The April ban by the US government on H20 GPU sales to China has materialized those risks. This week, the Trump administration has looked to increase export restrictions further. Nvidia took a massive inventory charge, and the stock has corrected by over 10% from its peak. In this follow up coverage, I want to reassess whether Q1 results and forward guidance help the company. Unfortunately, my initial read is that I don’t think there is a lot to like here.

Q1 Review: Slowing Growth With China Hurting

Nvidia reported its Q1 earnings on Wednesday, and while the company did beat expectations in the two key areas (EPS and revenue), it's clear their growth is decelerating rapidly. On Wednesday, the company reported a Non-GAAP EPS of $0.81, which beat the consensus estimate of $0.75 by $0.06. On this same front, the company finished the quarter with a revenue total of $44.06 billion, which beat the consensus by about $810 million and reflected a 69.2% year-over-year increase.

However, what’s concerning to me about this growth is actually the quarter-over-quarter growth, not the year-over-year growth. Of course, a 69.2% year-over-year increase for revenue is fantastic, but the quarter-over-quarter growth rate of just 12% is flat (compared to the 12% q/q growth) it posted in the quarter prior. What this means is that revenue is getting closer to becoming seasonal (some quarters are stagnant while others show strong growth). For a company with strong growth, every quarter books strong q/q growth.

As I mentioned before I think a big driver of this is the export-ban fallout. On April 9, the U.S. required licenses for H20 GPU exports to China. In response to this, Nvidia booked a $4.5 billion charge, “walked away” from ~$15 billion in potential China sales, and missed ~$2.5 billion worth of shipments in FQ1 alone.

In China, the (originally Biden and now Trump) administration’s crackdown on chip exports is causing homegrown rivals (like Huawei) to accelerate their AI chip development.

In the short run, the effect will be minimal. Nvidia’s sales to China are muted. In the long run, Huawei is a fierce competitor. I believe this could impact international sales as well (back in the US for example). I think this is the equivalent of a hardware deepseek for the US GPU market. The market is already moving towards TCO (total cost of ownership). Nvidia’s pricing model is not yet following.

In a statement provided in the Q1 earnings release, Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang spoke to the reliance that the company has on AI infrastructure.

Our breakthrough Blackwell NVL72 AI supercomputer — a ‘thinking machine’ designed for reasoning— is now in full-scale production across system makers and cloud service providers, said Huang in a statement.

Global demand for NVIDIA’s AI infrastructure is incredibly strong. AI inference token generation has surged tenfold in just one year, and as AI agents become mainstream, the demand for AI computing will accelerate. Countries around the world are recognizing AI as essential infrastructure — just like electricity and the internet — and NVIDIA stands at the center of this profound transformation.

While Nvidia’s margins were clearly distorted by one-offs I am concerned about what this can mean with more foreign competition here in the home market. The company’s GAAP gross margin fell to 60.5% (Non-GAAP 61.0%). Excluding the H20 charge, margins would be about 71.3%, which falls right in line with historical norms. Additionally, the adjusted EPS would have been $0.96, showing core profitability intact.

Interestingly, management is currently projecting a Q2 revenue of $45 billion +/- 2%, above consensus, which implies a modest sequential dip even after accounting for unshipped China orders. This will be a huge deal for the GPU giant to book a sequential dip. This is below the guidance estimate of $45.66 billion.

Keep in mind we're seeing a sharp deceleration from 12% q/q growth to near negative q/q growth.

Valuation

As I’ve written multiple times before on the GPU giant, Nvidia continues to trade at a premium to the sector median on most key valuation metrics. As I have dove into before, I think this centers around their P/E ratio. With a forward Non-GAAP price-to-earnings ratio, we can see that Nvidia has a forward P/E ratio of 32.59. When we compare this to the sector median of 21.78, this is a 49.61% premium to the sector median. Seeking Alpha Quant gives Nvidia a grade of a C- on this metric. In my opinion, this premium demands sustained outperformance. Unfortunately, growth is slowing down.

In terms of sales, Nvidia’s Trailing Twelve Months price-to-sales ratio of 22.97 dwarfs competitor AMD’s (AMD) ratio of 6.60. Nvidia’s mark of 22.97 is 676.49% higher than the sector median of 2.96. Seeking Alpha Quant gives Nvidia a grade of an F on this metric.

Nvidia’s TTM EV/EBITDA ratio of 37.99 is nearly double the sector median of 16.84, and this is given a grade of a D+, according to Seeking Alpha Quant. In my view, current multiples underprice China risks especially with homegrown innovation. The potential for $3-4.5 billion quarterly revenue loss under strict export rules isn’t fully baked in, and local competitors like Huawei and its 910C GPU will continue to erode share. CEO Huang explicitly talked about that on the call.

With this, I think we need to see shares converge on the sector median in order to fully price in this risk. If we saw shares converge on the sector median forward P/E of 21.78, this would represent 33.16% downside for shares.

Bull Thesis

While this is my second bearish article on Nvidia in the last week, in a spirit of full transparency I still do think there is a bull thesis.

Any bull thesis begins with the Secular AI demand. While Chinese competitors are popping up, global AI compute demand is still red-hot. Cloud hyperscalers and enterprises are racing to buy every chip Nvidia can make, and even ex-China, other regions are stepping up. Nvidia has done a lot of promotion in the Middle East this year for GPUs.

With this, I do think that Nvidia has built itself a nice moat in both software and hardware on the pre-training side. The company has a full-stack offering (Blackwell architecture + CUDA ecosystem) that creates high switching costs. The key, however, (as I have outlined before) is that the future is about TCO and inference not about pre-training.

Really, the issue is that the market they are playing in is the one that is plateauing, where as the inference market that is accelerating is more focused on the TCO acronym I keep using. IT infrastructure almost always converges on cost optimizations as the technology matures. What we're seeing with the AI GPU market is no different.

Nvidia's growth has been nothing short of remarkable. And while they are still finding pockets of growth, I think the overall growth story has slowed down a ton. With any company running at 44 billion in quarterly revenue, it takes a lot to move the needle. Nvidia is no different. The problem is that (as a hardware business) it's taking a lot just to maintain this same level they're currently at.

Takeaway

Under difficult circumstances, I will admit Nvidia still was able to deliver a solid quarter. The company beat Q1 expectations but guided below fear-driven expectations for revenue. Overall, AI demand appears resilient, but that doesn’t mean that competition risks from China won’t be huge.

Despite this strong quarter, the era of triple-digit growth is clearly over. Nvidia will be hard pressed to put up quarterly performances like it has in the past, and that became evident with the fall off in quarter-over-quarter growth that the company experienced when earnings were released on Wednesday. While year over year growth has fallen off, their P/E has not declined a similar percentage. This tells me shares are mispriced. I think there is downside from here.

While the long-term story for AI remains strong, I think the near-term risk/reward ratio (especially for Nvidia) is way less compelling. Because of this, I still believe that shares of Nvidia are a strong sell.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Unchanged From April, Still Near Record Lows
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment was unchanged from April, ending four straight months of declines. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index remained at 52.2 in May, the fourth-lowest reading on record. The latest reading was higher than the 50.8 preliminary reading from earlier in the month, highlighting increased optimism during the latter half of the month. Consumer sentiment has fallen by 24.5% (16.9 points) compared to a year ago.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regards to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month; a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment was unchanged from April, ending four consecutive months of plunging declines. Sentiment had ebbed at the preliminary reading for May but turned a corner in the latter half of the month following the temporary pause on some tariffs on China goods. Expected business conditions improved after mid-month, likely a consequence of the trade policy announcement. However, these positive changes were offset by declines in current personal finances stemming from stagnating incomes throughout May. Overall, consumers see the outlook for the economy as no worse than last month, but they remained quite worried about the future.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 52.2, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put Friday’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 38.2% below its average reading of 84.5 (arithmetic mean) and 37.4% below its geometric mean of 83.3, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 1st percentile of the 569 monthly data points in this series.

This indicator is somewhat volatile, with an average monthly change of 3.1 points. The latest data shows a 0.0-point change from the previous month. To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average. The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In May, the CECI dropped to 58.9, the lowest reading since November 2022. This represents an 1.5% decline from the previous month and a 15.4% decline from a year ago. This reading was above the forecast of 57.6.

Meanwhile, the CEI inched up to 47.9. This represents a 1.3% increase from the prior month and a 30.4% drop from one year ago. This reading was above the forecast of 46.5.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations were little changed at 6.6%, inching up from 6.5% last month. This is the smallest increase since the election and marks the end of a four-month streak of extremely large jumps in short-run expectations. Notably, long-run inflation expectations fell back from 4.4% in April to 4.2% in May. This is the first decline seen since December 2024 and ends an unprecedented four-month sequence of increases. Given that consumers generally expect tariffs to pass through to consumer prices, it is no surprise that trade policy has influenced consumers’ views of the economy. In contrast, despite the many headlines about the tax and spending bill that is moving through Congress, the bill does not appear to be salient to consumers at this time.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include: Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Hess Midstream: A Compelling Opportunity Despite Recent Noise
data.description:
Shares of Hess Midstream (NYSE:HESM) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining 6% while also paying a 7+% dividend yield. That said, shares do remain well below their pre-Liberation Day highs, as oil prices have been weighed down by increasing OPEC+ production. This has a limited medium-term impact on HESM given its fee-based contracts, making the sell-off overdone in my view, even with some recent legal headlines. I last covered shares in January, rating the stock a “strong buy,” but since then it has lost 5% despite once approaching my $45 price target. With recent news, now is a good time to see if HESM can regain momentum.

Before looking at its recent financial performance, there have been some recent developments that merit attention. Most notably, on May 20th, Continental Resources sued Hess Corp (HES), arguing that it was defrauded out of up to $69 million. It had a non-operating interest in 483 producing wells, and Hess Midstream provides midstream services for these wells. CLR is arguing that Hess Corp pays too much for these midstream services. Because HES owns ~38% of HESM, it is less sensitive to over-paying for fees since it recoups some of this via its ownership. However because non-operating partners do not have a stake in HESM, they are essentially overpaying with HES than receiving some of this overpayment via its distributions from HESM, or so CLR alleges.

I think it is important to note that Hess and not Hess Midstream is the counterparty to this lawsuit, so HESM has no legal liability as a result. As I have discussed in my past coverage of HESM, its contracts with Hess are favorable, offering secure fee-based revenue with strong minimum volume guarantees. These contracts were clearly set up to support HESM’s valuation in public markets upon its spin out from Hess. HESM has been public for nearly 8 years, and its contract structure is well known.

Any entity that chooses to own a stake in a Hess well knows what the contract terms are and what the takeaway costs are. Plus while Hess has a stake in HESM, it is a minority stake. To the extent it overpays, 62% of this benefit leaves the company, limiting how much of a benefit Hess would want to provide HESM. While Hess Corp may have modest legal liability, I see no liability to HESM or any material risk of a restructuring of contracts.

Hess Midstream’s contract runs through 2033, so there is over 7 years of runway to its structure. After that, contract terms can change, but ultimately Hess Corp and Midstream are dependent on each other. Hess Corp accounts for 90% of the volume on Midstream’s system, as noted on the last earnings call. At the same time, Hess Corp is dependent on Midstream’s network of pipes to move its oil and gas to the market.

Under its current contract, HESM takes no commodity risk, earning a fee for moving product, and this fee generally rises alongside CPI. These terms are not unusual; typically, midstream providers have limited commodity risk. While HESM takes volume risk, this is mitigated by minimum volume commitments (MVC), which protect 80% of revenue. Basically, Hess has to pay for a minimum amount of usage, even if it falls short. This feature is not all that abnormal, as pipeline companies need some certainty of revenue to build out their infrastructure.

What is unique is that these volumes are reset every year, for three years out at 80% of production levels. Additionally for oil and gas, they can never decline. This protects not only 80% of prior revenue but also 80% of incremental revenue growth. This rising MVC structure through 2033 combined with its flat-fee payment is why HESM has one of the cleanest revenue and distribution growth stories in the sector.

Now, I would note that production is running above these levels, so the MVC levels are not in force. I view this as a positive. If a pipeline is being fully used, the producer is very likely to sign a new contract when the existing one expires. Conversely, if productions is consistently below the MVC, the MVC provides helpful current cash flow, but the pipeline company will likely struggle to renew contracts. I always hope to see HES produce in excess of its MVC threshold, but these do provide a substantial floor to HESM’s income. As you can see, production has steadily grown over several years, supporting HESM’s growth and a rising long-term MVC level.

Now, if we do see oil prices fall more meaningfully for longer, we could see Hess Corp pull back on its drilling activity, though for now, it continues to run 4 rigs, which should support stable to growing production. We also continue to await the completion of Chevron’s (CVX) acquisition of Hess, which requires completion of an arbitration fight with Exxon (XOM) over the Guyana asset. I do not see the closure of this deal as likely to shift Bakken production, though as I have discussed in the past, CVX could eventually try to acquire HESM as it did with Noble’s midstream entity after closing the deal for Noble.

Now, in the company’s first quarter reported on April 30th, Hess Midstream earned $0.65, which was $0.04 below expectations even as revenue grew by 7% to $382 million. The company generated $292 million of adjusted EBITDA, up $18 million from last year. Its $191 million of free cash flow was flat to last year as it invested $15 million more in cap-ex. HESM is completing some growth cap-ex projects this year, so cap-ex is running at the high-end of its $250-$300 million medium-term target. Of this, about $100-125 million is sustaining work with the remainder funding growth projects. EBITDA margins remain very strong at 82%.

Q1 results were negatively impacted by harsh winter conditions in January and February, which can reduce wellhead performance. Gas volumes were weak at 424mmcf/d vs full year guidance of 455-465. This was down about 5% from last quarter but up about 8% from last year. Crude volumes were down 2% sequentially at 117bpd but up 10% from last year. Very cold weather in January and February led to weather-related outages, but production has rebounded well and came out of March strong. As such, it reaffirmed guidance. For all of the concern about lower prices hurting production, we continue to see solid year-on-year growth, and better natural gas prices do help to offset weaker crude oil pricing.

Hess Midstream remains on track to generate over $735 million of free cash flow, and as a result, alongside results, it increased its distribution by 1.2% to $0.7098. Its balance sheet remains one of the best in the sector with 3.1x debt/EBITDA leverage. It targets 3x debt/EBITDA, and I expect it to end this year at or below that level given sequential EBITDA growth.

Given this balance sheet strength, we have seen HESM actively buy back shares. On May 6th, Hess Midstream announced a $200 million share repurchase, buying $10 million of public shares and $190 million from its sponsors, Hess and Global Infrastructure Partners at $36.88. Continuing to buy back units with a yield above 7% should support incremental distribution increases for remaining units. As you can see, HESM has been buying back about $100 million in shares per quarter over the few years.

Management expects to have about $1.25 billion of incremental capital return capacity between now and the end of 2027, assuming 5% annual distribution increases. About $500-600 million of this comes from retained free cash flow. The other $600-700 million will come from the fact that rising EBITDA will reduce leverage, creating incremental borrowing capacity to buy back shares. We are likely to see HESM retire 10-15% of its units over the next 2.5 years. This lower unit count is one reason I believe distribution growth is likely to be closer to 7% than 5%.

With a starting yield of 7.4%, I view this as a very attractive opportunity, as HESM is well positioned to deliver ~14% long-term returns (yield + distribution growth). I continue to believe its contract structure securely insulates it from macro pressure and do not see material risk to this structure, despite recent headlines. Additionally, if the CVX-HES deal is completed, I believe this would likely lead to an acquisition of HESM by CVX at a premium. I continue to see HESM as a strong buy and believe shares can return to their pre-Liberation Day levels, towards $45.

data.title: Sezzle: Buy If You Want Your Money To Fizzle
data.description:
Introduction

Sezzle Inc. (NASDAQ:SEZL) is a fintech company that operates in the Buy Now, Pay Later space. They offer payment solutions in-store and online at a variety of retail brands, and they provide proprietary solutions that connect consumers with instant lines of credit at the point of sale. Most of their loans are Pay in 4 installments, but they also have other cadences available to customers. The company also offers a virtual card and a variety of other products in its portfolio that cater to a variety of customers. Since their inception, they have transacted over $10B in gross merchandise volume and have over 16M sign ups in users.

SEZL has ballooned over the past 2 years in share price. Year to date, the shares are up ~145%. Since May 2024, the stock is up over 860%. This is outstanding performance, driven by strong user growth and beneficial partnerships, but at this point, with shares at all-time highs, we believe that it is time to short the company.

It is now time to sell SEZL for 2 main reasons. Firstly, the current macro environment lends itself to a poor time for BNPL names. Secondly, there are increasing concerns with valuation that should be highlighted.

Market Dynamics are shifting against SEZL

In their May 2025 investor presentation, SEZL notes that a core part of their target market are younger credit consumers. However, and they explicitly say this in the materials, younger consumers struggle with below-average credit scores.

The issue with going after this customer base is simple. Despite being the segment that could most use and benefit from BNPL and breaking up purchases into installments, there is a reason their credit score is lower - they have not proven to be reliable borrowers in the past. Whether that is an indication or not of how trustworthy they will be in the future on their loans and credit is to be determined, but credit score is a massive financial indicator in this country. According to SEZL, 78% of their consumers are between the ages of 18-47, and 71% are using BNPL products to better manage their cash flows. Sure - this is a great product idea in the sense that it does allow more consumers to purchase products that they may not have the budget for in the first place, but the issue remains that just because someone can afford the first payment or two, does not mean this group of consumers will continue to make payments / afford the ongoing payments.

Furthermore, SEZL notes that nearly half (48%) of their customers are individuals who have been rejected for a credit product, at least once, in the past year. This raises a red flag for us - if big banks and credit unions, with their myriad of creditworthiness analytical products, say no to a customer, why is SEZL taking on the leftovers / rejects? While credit score is not the end all be all to how financially strong a customer may be, it definitely paints an accurate depiction. Remember the last time everyone started going after subprime customers? That did not end too well.

This hypothesis appears to be playing out in reality. Klarna, a private competitor, just announced that their net losses have doubled in the first quarter even though their revenue and user count grew. A major reason is that across the industry, consumers are starting to default and miss payments on their balances. LendingTree states that 41% of users in the past month have paid a loan late, up from 34% in the previous year. This is alarming. Additionally, over 25% of users have started taking out loans to pay for groceries, an absolute necessity. This is equally alarming. As the job market continues to waver, M&A activity remains stagnant, interest rates stay high, and other factors that impact the financial flexibility of this target segment (e.g., continuation of student loan repayment) we expect the average consumer to be tightened even more financially, which means more users for BNPL but likely more losses and late payments as well. When the next revenue stream for BNPL names is fast food purchases, one must wonder if things are getting too out of hand. For SEZL, after a year of incredible share appreciation, these market factors are holding up a large sign saying pause.

Competitors

As prudent investors, before we make any investment decisions on any given equity, we look at its competitors. We see competitors as potential alternatives that have similar businesses and macro impacts but possibly at a cheaper cost. For this analysis, we look at PayPal (PYPL), Block (XYZ) and Affirm (AFRM).

Let’s start with the most common approach: P/E ratio.

AFRM does not show a P/E as it is not profitable yet. However, you can see that SEZL's P/E is very high compared to the rest, at around 2x of PYPL and XYZ.

Now let’s look at P/S.

SEZL once again stands out from the pack, but not in a good way. It has roughly 2x the P/S ratio of the next highest competitor (AFRM).

Now, a higher valuation on a couple of metrics does not always mean that a company is overvalued. There could be other factors at play such as book value, cash flows or growth. In the case of SEZL, a fast-growing company, we are particularly interested in seeing if revenue growth can justify the lofty valuation.

This chart shows how the revenues of each company have grown since 2021. SEZL has not always had the highest growth but in the last FY 2024 especially, they stood out from the pack. Adding up the total grown from FY 2021 through FY 2024 we see:

This time, SEZL also stands out but now in a good way. They have the highest growth out of the pack at 136% with AFRM not too far behind at 106%. Does this mean that SEZLs high valuation is justified? We think maybe it is when compared to PYPL or XYZ. However, we are not so sure about AFRM.

AFRM has roughly 4.5x the revenue of SEZL at $1.5 billion in the TTM, compared to SEZLs $329 million. Additionally, while AFRM is not profitable like SEZL, they are approaching profitability with only a net loss of $62.2 million in the TTM compared to a loss of $517.8 million and $985.3 million in the last 2 FYs. Finally, AFRM has a large cash position of $1.4 billion that can help fuel their continual growth and market reach and a healthy current ratio of 13.47. SEZL on the other hand only has $88.9 million in cash and a current ratio of 2.62.

Overall, it is clear that in terms of valuation metrics, SEZL is quite overvalued compared to peers. Some of this over valuation could be explained by SEZL’s high growth, but it does not fully justify it, especially when compared to AFRM.

Risks to the thesis

There are 2 risks to the thesis that should be highlighted.

Valuation Improves: SEZL has shown high growth as we have seen, and they have recently achieved positive net income and are not showing signs of slowing down. One risk to our thesis is that SEZL continues to show high growth, beating out expectations significantly. Overall, we do not see this as a huge risk. The company in its Q1 earnings call revised 2025 revenue growth to 60-65% which in the best case (65%) would mean that 2025 revenues will be $447.3 million compared to the 2024 revenue of $271.1 million. The reported P/S of 11.3 was based on the company’s TTM revenue, which was $329.1 million. So doing some rough math to calculate the P/S with $447.3 million revenue:

(11.3 * 329.1 million) / 447.3 million = 8.31At a P/S of 8.31, SEZL will still be overvalued compared to AFRM's 5.45 P/S by ~1.5x. Our conclusion: even SEZL’s best-case revenue scenario will result in a relatively higher valuation compared to competitors.

M&A Activity from a Larger Financial Player: If SEZL becomes part of M&A talks or activity, that would quickly boost the stock price further, leading to the thesis being wrong. There have been no active or recent mentions of any sort of activity like this being discussed at the moment, but it is always something that a smaller (by market cap) fintech like this could be engaged with. Players who could be looking to acquire SEZL include large banks, competing BNPL firms looking to merge, and possibly, if the company can continue to show steady financials, private equity firms focused on the consumer tech sector. However, with M&A activity still not really picking up yet, this remains a lower risk.

Conclusion

To summarize, SEZL has grown astronomically over the past year or so, and it’s time that investors cool off on buying this stock. The current macro dynamics of US consumers feeling unstable, paired with the fact that SEZL targets a less than prime segment of the consumer market, means that they would be more exposed to consumers pulling back on spending / missing payments. As mentioned above, with firms like Klarna, signs of consumers missing payments and leading to losses are already starting to show.

In addition to the macro risks, SEZL is also demonstrably overvalued compared to its peers, so an investor interested in investing in buy now, pay later likely has better options.

For now, SEZL is a sell.

data.title: IEEPA Tariffs On Hold Following Court Ruling
data.description:
The US Court of International Trade (CIT) has rejected the Trump administration’s use of the International Economic Emergency Powers Act to apply the “Worldwide,” “Retaliatory” (CIT definition) and “Trafficking” tariff plans. The CIT ruled the Executive Orders enacting the tariffs “are declared to be invalid” as they “exceed the authority of the president to regulate importation by means of tariffs.”

The order, which takes the form of a summary ruling, gave the administration 10 calendar days to put its order into action. The Department of Justice appealed the ruling to the Court of Appeals for the Federal Circuit, which in turn has ordered the tariffs remain in place while the appellate process continues.

Depending on the outcome of the Court of Appeals review, the administration may also appeal to the Supreme Court for a final ruling in an attempt to quicken the judicial process. A delay in the repeal of the tariffs could be as long as several months as the judicial process unfolds.

Normally, federal cases can take years to reach the Supreme Court, with the high court often sending cases back to lower courts for further clarifications. However, due to the importance of the CIT decision, the appeal process could be resolved in a matter of weeks or months.

If the appeal court stays the CIT decision, leaving the current tariffs in place until it reaches a decision, a delay in the repeal of the tariffs could be as long as several months as the judicial process unfolds.

If the IEEPA tariffs are ultimately removed, the administration is likely to look for alternative mechanisms to apply the tariffs. These could include reframing the IEEPA tariffs to meet the CIT’s concerns or wider use of sector-specific investigations using the Section 232 (national security) or Section 201 (safeguarding) review processes, among other options.

The CIT ruling does not include the Section 232 reviews of seven industries that are underway, nor the three already in place, which together account for 42% of US imports and were in any event excluded from the IEEPA tariffs.

Actions under the Section 232 review results could be brought forward to allow the administration to demonstrate continued action on tariffs, with the president recently flagging that tariffs on smartphones could be increased to 25% as soon as late June.

Should duties be removed, this would reduce the pressure on ongoing negotiations with countries to sign improved trade conditions. Part of the CIT’s ruling was based on the administration’s argument that IEEPA was being used to “pressure” countries into reaching a deal.

Continuing negotiations

At a minimum, the ruling makes the July 9 deadline for most countries and Aug. 12 for mainland China irrelevant, though negotiations may continue because of the chance that a Supreme Court appeal succeeds or that the CIT decision gets narrowed. That could prove particularly important for situations where negotiations are proving contentious or where elections are complicating the negotiating process.

Mexico’s President Claudia Sheinbaum is likely to continue tariff negotiations with the US despite the court suspension, particularly as she seeks to bring forward the review of the USMCA from 2026 to the second half of 2025. Separately, negotiations on the 25% duty on steel, aluminum and automotive imports are likely to continue, as these were not affected by this court process.

Countries with higher-than-average exposures to the existing and ongoing Section 232 reviews, including South Korea and Japan in the case of steel, aluminum, autos and electronics, and for the EU in regard to autos and pharmaceuticals, also have an incentive to continue negotiations.

Even with IEEPA relief, a significant portion of mainland Chinese exports would remain exposed to other tariff mechanisms (such as Section 232 or Section 301), should the Trump administration pursue them. Sectors where mainland China dominates global supply chains (including consumer electronics, telecommunications equipment or solar panels) would be particularly vulnerable to Section 232 national security reviews.

With regard to the overall impact on supply chains, the US administration still has other channels to increase import costs, so businesses may still not have enough certainty to plan their investments and production. The sectors that would benefit most from the IEEPA duties being overturned are consumer goods industries where mainland China has a high proportion of US imports.

Among the larger import sectors those include toys (75.8% imported from mainland China in 2024), homewares, and audiovisual equipment, among others. All three are also highly seasonal and face sourcing uncertainties heading into the peak shipping season, which typically involves exports from Asia running from June through September.

Thus far, companies have focused on tactical actions including inventory front-loading, price increases and cost negotiations, and may continue to do so during the period of tariff uncertainty.
